metadata.name: b2b-saas-gtm
metadata.description: Domain ontology for a B2B SaaS company's go-to-market operations. Models accounts, contacts, subscriptions, product usage, and engagement signals to power an AI agent that identifies revenue expansion opportunities, predicts churn risk, and recommends GTM plays.

entities.name: Account
entities.description: A company that is a customer, prospect, or former customer. Accounts are the primary commercial unit — all revenue, contracts, and engagement roll up to the account level.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Legal entity name or commonly used company name.

attributes.name: domain
attributes.description: Primary web domain (e.g., acme.com). Used for identity resolution and enrichment.

attributes.name: industry
attributes.description: Industry vertical per our ICP definition.

attributes.name: industry_seasonality
attributes.description: Whether this industry has predictable seasonal patterns that affect usage. Cyclical industries (retail, travel, construction) show usage dips that are NOT churn signals. The usage-decline-alert rule should adjust thresholds for cyclical accounts. See outcomes/q2-retrospective.lore for the false-positive analysis that motivated this field.

attributes.name: fiscal_year_end
attributes.description: When this account's fiscal year ends. Affects budget cycle timing — expansion pitches should land 2-3 months before fiscal year end when budget is being allocated.

attributes.name: employee_count
attributes.description: Most recent headcount from enrichment data.

attributes.name: segment
attributes.description: Determined by ARR band. Enterprise >= $100K ARR, Mid-market $25K-$100K, SMB < $25K.

attributes.name: arr
attributes.description: Current annualized recurring revenue from all active subscriptions. Updated on each billing cycle.

attributes.name: stage
attributes.description: Current lifecycle stage. See Lifecycle section.

attributes.name: health_score
attributes.description: Composite score reflecting engagement, usage, support sentiment, and commercial signals.

attributes.name: nrr_trailing_12m
attributes.description: Net revenue retention for this account over trailing 12 months. Values above 1.0 indicate expansion; below 1.0 indicate contraction.

attributes.name: csm_owner
attributes.description: The CSM responsible for this account. References our internal team member (modeled as Contact with is_internal = true).

attributes.name: ae_owner
attributes.description: The Account Executive who owns the commercial relationship.

attributes.name: icp_fit_score
attributes.description: How closely this account matches our Ideal Customer Profile. Based on industry, size, tech stack, and use case alignment.

entities.identity:
An account is uniquely identified by its primary web domain.
If two business entities share a domain, they are treated as
one account. Parent/subsidiary relationships are modeled via
the SUBSIDIARY_OF relationship, not as separate domain entries.
entities.lifecycle:
Accounts progress through stages based on commercial and
engagement milestones:

- **Prospect**: No signed contract. May have open opportunities.
- **Onboarding**: First contract signed, implementation in progress.
  Transition to Active requires: (1) product deployed in production,
  (2) at least 5 active users, (3) first value milestone confirmed.
- **Active**: Live customer with healthy engagement.
- **At-risk**: Health score dropped below 40 OR NRR < 0.85 OR
  champion departed. Triggers CSM intervention playbook.
- **Churned**: All subscriptions cancelled or expired. Grace period
  of 30 days before moving to Former.
- **Former**: Previously churned, now a win-back target.
entities.notes:
Account data is enriched weekly from Clearbit/ZoomInfo.
Revenue data syncs daily from the billing system (Stripe).
Stage transitions are automated by rules/scoring.lore but
can be manually overridden by CSM with a logged reason.

**Expected adoption curves by segment:**
- SMB: should reach adoption_score > 0.5 within 30 days.
  If not, the product is likely too complex for their team
  size. Recommend training or a simplified onboarding flow.
- Mid-market: 60-day window to reach 0.5 adoption. These
  accounts benefit most from implementation services.
- Enterprise: 90-day window is normal. Complex onboarding
  with multiple departments, IT review, SSO setup. Do not
  trigger adoption concern signals before 90 days.

**Budget cycle intelligence:**
- Expansion pitches land best 2-3 months before fiscal year
  end (when budget is being planned) or in Q1 of their fiscal
  year (when new budget is available).
- Avoid expansion conversations in the last month of their
  fiscal year — budget is already committed.
- If fiscal_year_end is unknown, assume Q4 (calendar year)
  unless the account is in government (Q3) or education (Q2).

**Cohort context:**
- Always compare an account's metrics to its cohort (same
  segment, similar onboarding date). An enterprise account
  with 0.4 adoption at 60 days is on track. An SMB account
  with 0.4 adoption at 60 days is falling behind.
- Peer benchmarks: the agent should know what "good" looks
  like for this account's segment and industry.

entities.name: Competitor
entities.description: A competing product or vendor that appears in our deal cycles and churn events. Competitors are not just companies — they include "do nothing" (stay on spreadsheets) and "build internally" (customer builds their own). Understanding competitor dynamics lets the agent craft targeted responses when a competitor-threat signal fires.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Competitor name as sales team refers to them.

attributes.name: type
attributes.description: Direct: head-to-head feature overlap. Adjacent: different category but solves similar problem. DIY: customer builds internally with spreadsheets/code. Inertia: "do nothing" — the status quo.

attributes.name: segment_strength
attributes.description: Where this competitor is strongest. Informs which of our accounts are most at risk.

attributes.name: primary_differentiator
attributes.description: What this competitor does that we don't, or does better. Must be honest — the agent needs accurate intel, not marketing spin.

attributes.name: our_advantage
attributes.description: What we do better than this competitor. Used to craft positioning when competitor-threat signal fires.

attributes.name: typical_displacement_trigger
attributes.description: What typically causes an account to consider switching to this competitor. Knowing the trigger helps us intervene before the evaluation starts.

attributes.name: win_rate_against
attributes.description: Historical win rate in deals where this competitor was present. Updated quarterly from closed-won/closed-lost data.

attributes.name: loss_reasons
attributes.description: Top reasons we lose to this competitor. Ordered by frequency.

attributes.name: counter_play
attributes.description: The recommended response when this competitor appears in a deal. Not a script — a strategic framework for how to position.

entities.identity:
A competitor is identified by name. Aliases should be tracked
(e.g., "SFDC" = "Salesforce") so the NLP pipeline can detect
mentions across all naming variants.
entities.notes:
Competitor intelligence is perishable. What was true about a
competitor 6 months ago may not be true today — they ship
features too. The agent should treat competitor data with
time-decayed confidence and flag entries older than 90 days
for review.

Current competitive landscape:

**CompeteFlow** (direct, mid-market strength)
- Their workflow automation is more visual (drag-and-drop).
- We win on analytics depth, API, and enterprise security.
- Win rate: 55%. We lose when the buyer is non-technical and
  values ease-of-setup over power.
- Counter: demo our workflow builder improvements, emphasize
  analytics moat, get a technical evaluator in the room.

**DataStack** (direct, enterprise strength)
- Enterprise-first, heavy on compliance and data governance.
- More expensive. Their analytics are stronger than ours.
- We win on time-to-value and mid-market fit.
- Win rate: 40%. We lose on compliance features in regulated industries.
- Counter: emphasize faster onboarding (weeks vs months),
  highlight total cost including their expensive implementation.

**Spreadsheets / Manual process** (diy, all segments)
- The #1 competitor across all segments. 40% of lost deals
  are to "no decision" — they stick with what they have.
- Typically: Excel/Sheets + email + manual process.
- We win when someone quantifies the time cost.
- Counter: ROI calculator, "what does this process cost you
  in hours per week?" framing. Find the person who's tired
  of maintaining the spreadsheet.

**Internal Tools** (diy, enterprise)
- Engineering team builds a custom internal tool.
- We lose to this when the company has spare engineering capacity
  and the champion is a CTO who prefers in-house.
- We win when maintenance cost becomes real (usually 6-12 months).
- Counter: "build vs buy" TCO analysis. Highlight ongoing
  maintenance, opportunity cost, and feature velocity gap.

entities.name: Contact
entities.description: A person associated with an account. Can be an external stakeholder (customer) or internal team member. Contacts are the human nodes in the relationship graph — deals, engagement, and influence flow through people.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Full name as it appears in CRM.

attributes.name: email
attributes.description: Primary email address. Used for identity resolution. @pii

attributes.name: title
attributes.description: Current job title.

attributes.name: role
attributes.description: Functional role in the buying/retention process. Champion: internal advocate who drives adoption. Economic buyer: controls budget and signs contracts. Blocker: actively resists expansion or renewal.

attributes.name: department
attributes.description: Organizational department (Engineering, Marketing, etc.)

attributes.name: seniority
attributes.description: Level in org hierarchy. Affects influence weight.

attributes.name: is_internal
attributes.description: True if this contact is our employee (CSM, AE, SE). Internal contacts are modeled as Contacts so they can participate in relationships like OWNS and ENGAGED_WITH.

attributes.name: last_engaged
attributes.description: Timestamp of most recent interaction (email, meeting, login).

attributes.name: engagement_score
attributes.description: Composite score based on recency, frequency, and depth of interactions.

attributes.name: champion_strength
attributes.description: For contacts with role = champion, how strong is their advocacy? Based on internal mentions, feature requests, reference willingness, executive access.

entities.identity:
A contact is uniquely identified by email address.
If a person has multiple email addresses, the primary
(work) email is canonical. Personal emails are stored
as aliases but do not create separate contact records.
entities.notes:
Contact enrichment runs via Clearbit on creation and
monthly thereafter. Title and seniority changes are
tracked — a champion who gets promoted is a strong
expansion signal; a champion who leaves is a churn risk.

entities.name: Feature
entities.description: A distinct capability within the platform that can be independently tracked for adoption, gated by tier, and used as a signal for expansion or stickiness. Features are the atomic unit of value delivery — the bridge between what we sell (Products) and what customers actually use (Usage).

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Feature name as it appears in the product UI and docs.

attributes.name: slug
attributes.description: Internal identifier used in telemetry events (e.g., "workflow-automation", "custom-sql-query").

attributes.name: category
attributes.description: Functional category. Used for grouping adoption metrics.

attributes.name: available_in
attributes.description: Which product tiers include this feature. E.g., ["professional", "enterprise"] means not in Starter.

attributes.name: is_aha_moment
attributes.description: Whether this feature is an "aha moment" — a capability that, once adopted, dramatically increases retention. Aha moment features count 3x in adoption_score calculation.

attributes.name: is_tier_gate
attributes.description: Whether this feature is a primary reason accounts upgrade to the next tier. Tier-gate features, when requested by a lower-tier account, are strong upgrade signals.

attributes.name: stickiness_score
attributes.description: How much adopting this feature increases retention. Based on historical cohort analysis. 1.0 = once adopted, account never churns. Updated quarterly by data team.

attributes.name: adoption_prerequisite
attributes.description: Feature slug that should be adopted first. Features have a natural adoption sequence — e.g., "basic-reporting" should be adopted before "cohort-analysis".

entities.identity:
Features are uniquely identified by slug. The slug matches
the telemetry event namespace so we can directly link feature
adoption tracking to the ontology.
entities.lifecycle:
Features go through maturity stages:
- **Beta**: available to opt-in accounts, not in official tier.
- **GA (General Availability)**: included in tier, fully supported.
- **Sunset**: being replaced, existing users grandfathered.

Feature lifecycle changes should be tracked as observations
because they affect signal interpretation — a usage decline
in a sunsetting feature is not a churn signal.
entities.notes:
The current feature inventory:

**Core features** (all tiers):
- Dashboard (`dashboard`) — the home screen, always adopted
- Team inbox (`team-inbox`) — shared communication
- Basic reporting (`basic-reporting`) — standard charts and tables
- Contact management (`contact-mgmt`) — CRM-like contact records
- Task management (`task-mgmt`) — assignment and tracking

**Analytics features** (Professional+):
- Advanced analytics (`advanced-analytics`) — @aha_moment
- Cohort analysis (`cohort-analysis`) — requires advanced-analytics
- Funnel visualization (`funnel-viz`) — requires advanced-analytics
- Custom SQL queries (`custom-sql-query`) — Analytics add-on only
- Predictive dashboards (`predictive-dashboards`) — Analytics add-on only
- Scheduled reports (`scheduled-reports`) — Analytics add-on only

**Automation features** (Professional+):
- Workflow automation (`workflow-automation`) — @aha_moment, @tier_gate
- Custom fields (`custom-fields`)
- Conditional logic (`conditional-logic`) — requires workflow-automation
- Approval chains (`approval-chains`) — requires workflow-automation

**Integration features** (varies by tier):
- Native integrations (`native-integrations`) — 5 on Starter, 25 on Pro, unlimited on Enterprise
- API access (`api-access`) — @tier_gate, Professional+ only
- Webhooks (`webhooks`) — Professional+
- Data Export (`data-export`) — Data Export add-on only, @aha_moment

**Security & Admin features** (Enterprise only):
- SSO/SAML (`sso-saml`) — @tier_gate, #1 reason for Enterprise upgrade
- Audit logging (`audit-log`) — @tier_gate, compliance requirement
- Custom data retention (`data-retention`) — Enterprise only
- Role-based access control (`rbac`) — basic in Pro, advanced in Enterprise

The three highest-impact features for expansion:
1. Workflow automation — once a team automates their first process,
   they build more. This is the feature that makes Professional sticky.
2. API access — signals that the account is integrating deeply.
   API-active accounts have 85% retention.
3. SSO/SAML — the #1 Enterprise upgrade trigger. When security/IT
   asks about SSO, the upgrade conversation has already started.

entities.name: Interaction
entities.description: Any engagement touchpoint between us and an account's contacts. Interactions are the raw signal layer — patterns in interactions reveal expansion readiness, churn risk, and relationship health.

attributes.name: type
attributes.description: Channel of interaction.

attributes.name: direction
attributes.description: Did the customer initiate or did we?

attributes.name: timestamp
attributes.description: When the interaction occurred.

attributes.name: participants
attributes.description: All contacts involved (both internal and external).

attributes.name: sentiment
attributes.description: Detected sentiment. For emails and tickets, derived via NLP. For meetings, from call transcript analysis.

attributes.name: topics
attributes.description: Key topics discussed. Extracted from transcripts, email body, or ticket tags.

attributes.name: summary
attributes.description: Brief summary of the interaction. Auto-generated from transcripts or email content.

attributes.name: is_executive
attributes.description: True if any participant has seniority = vp or c-level. Executive engagement is a strong health signal.

entities.notes:
Interaction frequency patterns are more predictive than
absolute counts. An account that typically has 10 interactions
per month dropping to 3 is a stronger churn signal than an
account that always has 3 per month.

Support ticket interactions with negative sentiment that
mention "competitor" or "alternative" in topics should
trigger immediate CSM alert regardless of health score.

entities.name: Opportunity
entities.description: A potential revenue event — new business, expansion, or renewal. Opportunities track the commercial pipeline and are the unit of GTM forecasting.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Descriptive name (e.g., "Acme Corp - Enterprise Upgrade Q2").

attributes.name: type
attributes.description: The commercial motion this opportunity represents.

attributes.name: stage
attributes.description: Current pipeline stage.

attributes.name: amount
attributes.description: Projected ARR value if closed-won.

attributes.name: close_date
attributes.description: Expected close date.

attributes.name: probability
attributes.description: Win probability based on stage and engagement signals.

attributes.name: source
attributes.description: How this opportunity was generated. Agent-recommended means the AI expansion agent identified and proposed it.

attributes.name: products
attributes.description: Which products are included in this opportunity.

attributes.name: primary_contact
attributes.description: Main point of contact for this deal.

attributes.name: champion
attributes.description: Internal champion advocating for this deal.

attributes.name: blockers
attributes.description: Known obstacles to closing (budget freeze, competing priority, technical concern, etc.)

entities.notes:
Expansion opportunities are the primary output of the
revenue expansion agent. When the agent identifies an
expansion signal, it creates an opportunity recommendation
with pre-populated fields based on the signal analysis.

An opportunity without an identified champion has a
historically 3x lower close rate and should be flagged.

entities.name: Play
entities.description: A recommended GTM action in response to a signal or account state. Plays bridge the gap between "the agent detected something" and "here's what the human should do." They are prescriptive but not scripts — they require human judgment, account context, and relationship awareness to execute.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Play name as referenced by rules and signals.

attributes.name: trigger_signal
attributes.description: The signal type that typically activates this play. References taxonomies/signal-types.lore node names.

attributes.name: owner_role
attributes.description: Who is responsible for executing this play.

attributes.name: urgency
attributes.description: How quickly the play should be executed after trigger. Immediate: same business day. This-week: within 5 business days. This-quarter: fit into regular cadence. Opportunistic: execute when natural touchpoint arises.

attributes.name: segment_applicability
attributes.description: Which account segments this play applies to. E.g., ["enterprise", "mid-market"] means not for SMB.

attributes.name: prerequisites
attributes.description: What must be true before executing. E.g., "champion identified" or "usage data available for 30+ days."

attributes.name: success_metric
attributes.description: How to measure whether this play worked.

entities.notes:
Plays are NOT automated actions. The agent recommends a play;
a human decides whether and how to execute it. The agent should
include play context when creating signals so the CSM/AE
understands the "why" behind the recommendation.

Current playbook:

**Expansion Plays:**

*Proactive Seat Expansion* (trigger: Feature Ceiling Hit / seat utilization > 85%)
- Owner: AE | Urgency: this-week | Segments: all
- Approach: reach out before they hit the limit. Frame as
  "I noticed your team is growing — let's make sure you have
  room." Offer volume discount for annual commitment at
  higher seat count. Do NOT wait for them to ask.
- Success: seat expansion closed within 30 days.

*Tier Upgrade Conversation* (trigger: tier-upgrade-readiness)
- Owner: AE with CSM warm intro | Urgency: this-quarter | Segments: all
- Approach: CSM introduces the AE as someone who can help
  with the "next phase." Frame around unlocking value, not
  increasing spend. Lead with the 2-3 features they'd gain
  that directly map to their heaviest usage patterns.
  Never cold-pitch an upgrade — always tie to observed need.
- Prerequisites: adoption_score > 0.8, usage_trend = growing
- Success: upgrade opportunity created within 60 days.

*Analytics Add-On Pitch* (trigger: heavy basic reporting + CSV exports)
- Owner: CSM | Urgency: opportunistic | Segments: professional, enterprise
- Approach: during a regular check-in, ask "I noticed your
  team runs a lot of reports — would it save time if those
  were predictive and auto-delivered?" Position as a time-saver,
  not a cost. Offer a 14-day trial of Analytics.
- Prerequisites: 2+ months of active basic reporting
- Success: Analytics trial started within 30 days.

*Whitespace Discovery* (trigger: multi-signal-expansion)
- Owner: AE | Urgency: this-quarter | Segments: mid-market, enterprise
- Approach: full product-fit analysis. Map current usage to
  catalog gaps. Build a 12-month expansion plan with the
  champion. Position as strategic partnership, not upsell.
- Prerequisites: champion identified, health_score > 70
- Success: expansion opportunity with 2+ products created.

**Retention Plays:**

*Value Realization Check-In* (trigger: Usage Decline)
- Owner: CSM | Urgency: this-week | Segments: all
- Approach: do NOT lead with "we noticed your usage dropped."
  Instead: "I wanted to check in on your team's priorities
  this quarter — are there areas where we could help more?"
  Listen for: changed priorities, team turnover, lost champion,
  product frustration. Then address the real issue.
- Success: usage trend stabilizes within 30 days.

*Champion Succession* (trigger: Champion Departure)
- Owner: CSM | Urgency: immediate | Segments: all
- Approach: confirm the departure. Identify the 2-3 most
  engaged remaining contacts. Invest heavily in the strongest
  candidate — schedule 1:1, share success playbooks, make them
  feel important. If no internal candidate, request exec
  sponsor introduction to their replacement.
- Escalation: if no replacement identified in 30 days,
  CSM manager intervenes with executive outreach.
- Success: new champion identified within 30 days.

*Competitive Counter* (trigger: Competitor Mention)
- Owner: CSM + AE together | Urgency: immediate | Segments: all
- Approach: don't be defensive. Ask: "What prompted you to
  look at alternatives?" Listen to the real gap. If it's a
  feature gap, check roadmap. If it's price, do value analysis.
  If it's relationship, escalate. Pull competitive intel from
  the Competitor entity for specific counter-positioning.
- Prerequisites: competitor identified (check aliases)
- Success: account remains through next renewal.

*Renewal Preparation* (trigger: Renewal Approaching, 90 days out)
- Owner: CSM | Urgency: this-quarter (start 90 days before)
- Approach: build renewal case 90 days before renewal date.
  Compile: usage metrics, value delivered (time saved, outcomes),
  ROI narrative, and expansion opportunity. If health < 60,
  activate retention plays first. If health > 70, turn renewal
  into expansion conversation.
- Prerequisites: usage data for 3+ months
- Success: renewal closed at same or higher ARR.

entities.name: Product
entities.description: A product or plan that we sell. Products are the units of our catalog — accounts subscribe to products, and expansion happens when accounts add new products or upgrade existing ones. See glossary/products.lore for canonical definitions and taxonomies/product-catalog.lore for the full hierarchy.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Product name as it appears in pricing page and contracts.

attributes.name: sku
attributes.description: Internal product identifier.

attributes.name: tier
attributes.description: Pricing tier. Determines feature set and limits. See taxonomies/product-catalog.lore for full tier details.

attributes.name: base_price_monthly
attributes.description: List price per seat per month before discounts. Enterprise is annual-only: multiply by 12 × seat minimum.

attributes.name: pricing_model
attributes.description: How this product is billed. Core tiers are per-seat. Data Export is flat-fee. Services are one-time.

attributes.name: minimum_seats
attributes.description: Minimum seat count for this product. Enterprise = 50. Below this, the account should be on a lower tier.

attributes.name: category
attributes.description: Core platform is the main product. Add-ons extend it. Professional services are implementation/consulting.

attributes.name: available_with
attributes.description: Which tiers this product can be attached to. E.g., Analytics add-on: ["professional", "enterprise"]. Empty means available to all tiers.

attributes.name: features
attributes.description: Key feature slugs included in this product. Maps to Feature.slug for adoption tracking and whitespace analysis.

attributes.name: upgrade_path
attributes.description: The natural next product for accounts on this plan. Starter -> Professional -> Enterprise.

attributes.name: typical_sales_cycle_days
attributes.description: Average days from opportunity creation to close. Starter: 1 (self-serve), Professional: 14, Enterprise: 45.

attributes.name: attach_rate
attributes.description: Current penetration for add-on products within their eligible base. Updated quarterly by RevOps.

entities.identity:
Products are uniquely identified by SKU. Product names can
change (marketing rebranding) but SKUs are permanent.
entities.notes:
Expansion revenue can come from three motions:
1. **Seat expansion** (more users on same plan) — frictionless,
   often doesn't need AE involvement. Triggered by seat
   utilization > 85%.
2. **Tier upgrade** (Starter -> Professional -> Enterprise) —
   highest ACV impact but requires champion + business case.
   Triggered by feature ceiling, security requirements, or
   user count exceeding tier limits.
3. **Add-on attach** (Analytics or Data Export added) —
   medium ACV, targeted pitch. Triggered by usage patterns
   that indicate the account is doing manually what the
   add-on automates.

The agent should understand which motion fits each account
and recommend the right play. A Professional account with
50+ users and security requests is an Enterprise upgrade
candidate. A Professional account that runs 30 reports
a month is an Analytics attach candidate. Both are
"expansion" but the play is completely different.

Professional services are not recurring revenue but they
are a leading indicator of retention and expansion. The
agent should recommend services alongside product sales —
an Enterprise upgrade without implementation services
leads to 40% lower adoption at 90 days.

entities.name: Signal
entities.description: A detected event or pattern that indicates a change in account trajectory — either toward expansion or toward churn. Signals are the AI agent's primary input for generating recommendations.

attributes.name: type
attributes.description: Classification of the signal per taxonomies/signal-types.lore

attributes.name: strength
attributes.description: Confidence level based on signal clarity and historical predictive value.

attributes.name: direction
attributes.description: Whether this signal suggests growth or risk.

attributes.name: detected_at
attributes.description: When the signal was first detected.

attributes.name: source_data
attributes.description: Raw evidence that generated this signal. Could be a usage metric change, an email excerpt, a support ticket, or a product event.

attributes.name: status
attributes.description: Workflow status. New signals need review; actioned means a play has been triggered.

attributes.name: recommended_play
attributes.description: The GTM play the agent recommends based on this signal. References a playbook entry.

entities.notes:
Signals are ephemeral — they represent a point-in-time
observation. Multiple related signals may be clustered
into a "signal group" that tells a coherent story. For
example: usage spike + new department onboarded + executive
meeting requested = strong expansion signal cluster.

Signal detection runs daily. Historical signals older than
90 days that were never actioned are auto-archived.

entities.name: Subscription
entities.description: An active or historical contract between us and an account. Subscriptions are the revenue unit — ARR, expansion, and contraction are measured at the subscription level and aggregated to the account.

attributes.name: subscription_id
attributes.description: Billing system identifier (Stripe subscription ID).

attributes.name: plan
attributes.description: The product/plan this subscription covers.

attributes.name: status
attributes.description: Billing status from payment system.

attributes.name: arr_amount
attributes.description: Annualized value of this subscription.

attributes.name: start_date
attributes.description: Contract start date.

attributes.name: renewal_date
attributes.description: Next renewal date. Critical for renewal forecasting.

attributes.name: contract_term_months
attributes.description: Length of current contract term.

attributes.name: seats_purchased
attributes.description: Number of licensed seats.

attributes.name: seats_active
attributes.description: Number of seats with active usage in last 30 days.

attributes.name: seat_utilization
attributes.description: seats_active / seats_purchased. Below 0.6 is a contraction risk signal.

attributes.name: expansion_eligible
attributes.description: Whether this subscription is eligible for upsell or expansion based on plan type and contract terms.

entities.notes:
Multi-product accounts have multiple subscriptions. Revenue
movements (expansion, contraction, churn) are tracked as
changes between subscription snapshots taken monthly.
A subscription with seat_utilization < 0.5 that is within
90 days of renewal is a high-priority contraction risk.

entities.name: Usage
entities.description: Aggregated product usage metrics for an account over a time period. Usage is the strongest leading indicator of both expansion and churn — it reveals whether the customer is getting value from the product.

attributes.name: period
attributes.description: Aggregation period for this usage record.

attributes.name: period_start
attributes.description: Start of the measurement period.

attributes.name: active_users
attributes.description: Unique users with at least one meaningful session.

attributes.name: total_sessions
attributes.description: Total login sessions across all users.

attributes.name: feature_adoption
attributes.description: Which features were used this period. Compared against the account's entitled features to calculate adoption %.

attributes.name: api_calls
attributes.description: For platform accounts, total API calls. Indicates depth of integration.

attributes.name: data_volume_gb
attributes.description: Data processed or stored. Growth indicates deepening reliance on the platform.

attributes.name: adoption_score
attributes.description: Ratio of features used vs. features available on the account's current plan.

attributes.name: usage_trend
attributes.description: Direction of usage over the last 3 periods.

entities.notes:
Usage data is the most objective signal we have. Unlike
interactions (which can be gamed by an AE scheduling
unnecessary check-ins), usage directly measures value
delivery.

Key usage patterns to watch:
- "Aha moment" features: If an account starts using
  advanced analytics or workflow automation, they've
  crossed a stickiness threshold.
- Department spread: Usage from 1 department vs. 3+
  departments is a fundamentally different expansion
  posture.
- Weekend/off-hours usage indicates the product has
  become critical infrastructure, not just a nice-to-have.

relationships.name: HAS_SUBSCRIPTION
relationships.description: An account can have multiple subscriptions (multi-product). All subscriptions roll up to the account's total ARR.

relationships.name: HAS_CONTACT
relationships.description: Contacts belong to accounts. A contact can only belong to one account (if they move companies, a new contact record is created and linked via PREVIOUSLY_AT).

relationships.name: SUBSCRIBES_TO
relationships.description: Each subscription is for one product/plan. An account with multiple products has multiple subscriptions.

relationships.name: HAS_OPPORTUNITY
relationships.description: Opportunities are attached to accounts. An account can have multiple open opportunities simultaneously.

relationships.name: OWNS
relationships.description: Internal contacts (CSMs, AEs) own accounts. One person can own many accounts; an account has one CSM and one AE.

relationships.name: SUBSIDIARY_OF
relationships.description: Parent/subsidiary relationship. Revenue may be reported at parent level for enterprise rollups.

relationships.name: PARTICIPATED_IN
relationships.description: Contacts participate in interactions. Both internal (our team) and external (customer) contacts are linked.

relationships.name: HAS_USAGE
relationships.description: Usage records are per account per period. An account accumulates usage records over time forming a time series.

relationships.name: TRIGGERED
relationships.description: Signals are detected at the account level. An account can have many active signals simultaneously.

relationships.name: GENERATED_FROM
relationships.description: Some signals originate from specific interactions (e.g., a negative sentiment support ticket generates a churn signal). Links signal to its evidence.

relationships.name: GENERATED_FROM_USAGE
relationships.description: Some signals originate from usage patterns (e.g., usage spike generates an expansion signal).

relationships.name: RESULTED_IN
relationships.description: When a signal is actioned and an opportunity is created, this links the signal to the outcome. Used for measuring signal-to-pipeline conversion.

relationships.name: INCLUDES_FEATURE
relationships.description: Products include features. A feature can appear in multiple products (e.g., basic-reporting is in all tiers). The agent uses this to map what an account has access to versus what they're actually using.

relationships.name: HAS_ADOPTED
relationships.description: Tracks which features an account is actively using. "Adopted" means the feature was used by 2+ users in the last 30 days. The gap between INCLUDES_FEATURE (what they have) and HAS_ADOPTED (what they use) is the adoption gap. When any user at this account first used this feature. Users who used this feature in the last 30 days. Trial: used 1-2 times. Light: weekly. Moderate: daily by some users. Heavy: daily by most users.

relationships.name: COMPETES_WITH
relationships.description: Which of our products a competitor directly challenges. CompeteFlow competes with Professional. DataStack competes with Enterprise. "Spreadsheets" competes with Starter. What specifically overlaps (e.g., "workflow automation", "enterprise security", "basic project management").

relationships.name: LOST_TO
relationships.description: When a deal is closed-lost to a specific competitor. Critical for tracking competitive win/loss rates and understanding where we're weak. Primary reason for the loss (price, feature gap, etc.)

relationships.name: RECOMMENDS_PLAY
relationships.description: Links a detected signal to the recommended play. This replaces the free-text recommended_play field with a structured reference to the Play entity.

relationships.name: REQUIRES_FEATURE
relationships.description: Feature prerequisite chain. Cohort analysis requires advanced analytics. Conditional logic requires workflow automation. The agent uses this to understand adoption sequence — don't recommend an advanced feature if the prerequisite hasn't been adopted yet.

traversals.name: revenue-by-product
traversals.description: Answers: "What products does this account use and what's the revenue for each?"

traversals.name: account-portfolio
traversals.description: Answers: "What's the total book of business for this CSM/AE?" Used for workload balancing and quota tracking.

traversals.name: expansion-pipeline
traversals.description: Answers: "What expansion deals are in flight for this account or across the portfolio?"

traversals.name: parent-rollup
traversals.description: Answers: "What's the total ARR across the entire corporate hierarchy?" Important for enterprise pricing and executive engagement strategy.

traversals.name: signal-to-evidence
traversals.description: Answers: "What happened that triggered this signal, and who was involved?" Essential for CSM context before taking action on a signal.

traversals.name: account-engagement-timeline
traversals.description: Answers: "What's the full engagement history for this account?" Ordered by timestamp, gives a narrative view of the relationship.

traversals.name: signal-to-revenue
traversals.description: Answers: "How much pipeline and revenue have our signals generated?" Key metric for proving AI agent ROI.

traversals.name: feature-adoption-gap
traversals.description: Answers: "What features does this account have access to?" Compare against Account -[HAS_ADOPTED]-> Feature to find the adoption gap — features available but not used. Large adoption gaps in "aha moment" features are intervention opportunities (training, CSM check-in).

traversals.name: competitive-exposure
traversals.description: Answers: "Which competitors are relevant for this account based on what products they use?" When a competitor-threat signal fires, this traversal identifies which competitor and pulls the counter-play.

traversals.name: signal-to-play
traversals.description: Answers: "What should the CSM/AE do in response to this signal?" Direct link from detection to action.

traversals.name: win-loss-by-competitor
traversals.description: Answers: "What's our win/loss rate against each competitor?" Aggregated to update Competitor.win_rate_against quarterly.

traversals.name: feature-upgrade-path
traversals.description: Answers: "What's the natural adoption sequence for this account?" Used by the agent to recommend the NEXT feature to adopt, not skip ahead.

rules.name: champion-departure-alert
rules.trigger: Contact with role=champion becomes inactive or leaves
rules.condition:
Contact WHERE account = this
AND role = "champion"
AND (last_engaged < now() - 60d OR title changed)
rules.action:
Create Signal with type = "Champion Departure"
Set signal.strength = "strong"
Set signal.direction = "contraction"
Set account.stage = "at-risk" IF health_score < 60
Notify csm_owner immediately
rules.prose:
Champion departure is historically our #1 churn predictor.
  When detected, the CSM should:
  1. Confirm whether the champion has left or changed roles
  2. If left: identify and cultivate a new champion within 14 days
  3. If changed roles: assess whether new role still touches product
  4. Map the remaining stakeholder landscape — is there anyone
     else who can advocate for renewal?

  If no replacement champion is identified within 30 days,
  escalate to CSM manager for executive intervention.

rules.name: usage-decline-alert
rules.condition:
usage.usage_trend = "declining"
AND usage.active_users < 0.7 * avg(usage.active_users, last 3 months)
rules.action:
Create Signal with type = "Usage Decline"
Set signal.strength based on decline severity:
- >40% decline: "strong"
- 20-40% decline: "moderate"
Recommended play: "Value realization check-in — don't
lead with 'we noticed your usage dropped.' Instead ask
about their current priorities and whether the product
is aligned with what they need right now."

rules.name: competitor-threat
rules.condition:
Interaction WHERE account = this
AND topics includes any of ["competitor", known_competitor_names]
AND sentiment IN ["negative", "neutral"]
AND timestamp > now() - 30d
rules.action:
Create Signal with type = "Competitor Mention"
Set signal.strength = "strong"
Set signal.direction = "contraction"
Notify csm_owner AND ae_owner immediately
rules.prose:
Competitor mentions combined with negative sentiment are
  an acute threat. The response must be fast and strategic —
  not defensive. The play is to understand what gap the
  competitor appears to fill and address it directly.

rules.name: renewal-risk-scoring
rules.condition:
subscription.renewal_date < now() + 90d
AND subscription.status = "active"
Evaluate renewal risk based on composite factors:
- Seat utilization < 0.6 → high contraction risk
- Health score < 40 → high churn risk
- No champion identified → elevated risk
- No executive engagement in 90+ days → elevated risk
- NRR trailing 12m < 0.9 → prior contraction pattern
rules.action:
Calculate composite renewal risk score
If high risk: escalate to csm_owner + manager
If medium risk: add to CSM priority queue
Recommended play depends on primary risk factor

rules.name: multi-signal-expansion
rules.trigger: Multiple expansion signals detected within 30 days
rules.condition:
count(Signal WHERE account = this
AND direction = "expansion"
AND strength IN ["strong", "moderate"]
AND detected_at > now() - 30d) >= 2
rules.action:
Create Opportunity with type = "expansion"
Set opportunity.source = "agent-recommended"
Calculate recommended products via whitespace-analysis
Notify ae_owner with signal summary
rules.prose:
When multiple expansion signals cluster within a short
  window, it indicates a genuine shift in account trajectory,
  not just noise. The agent should synthesize the signals
  into a narrative: "Acme has added 15 new users from the
  marketing team this month, hit their API rate limit twice,
  and their VP of Engineering attended our webinar. This
  suggests cross-departmental adoption momentum."

rules.name: whitespace-analysis
rules.condition:
account.stage = "active"
AND account.health_score > 60
For each product in our catalog that the account does NOT
currently subscribe to, evaluate fit based on:
1. Are they using features that are precursors to this product?
(e.g., using basic reporting heavily -> analytics add-on)
2. Does their industry/segment typically adopt this product?
3. Have similar accounts expanded into this product?
4. Is there usage evidence of the need? (e.g., exporting
data to do externally what our add-on does natively)
rules.action:
Attach whitespace analysis to account record
Rank products by fit score
If top product fit > 0.7, create expansion signal

rules.name: seat-expansion-trigger
rules.action:
Create Signal with type = "Feature Ceiling Hit"
Set signal.strength = "strong"
Set signal.direction = "expansion"
Recommended play: "Proactive seat expansion offer —
reach out before they hit the limit. Offer volume
discount for committing to higher seat count."

rules.name: tier-upgrade-readiness
rules.trigger: Account shows pattern of needing next-tier features
rules.condition:
account.stage = "active"
AND subscription.plan.tier IN ["starter", "professional"]
AND usage.adoption_score > 0.8
AND usage.usage_trend = "growing"
AND account.health_score > 70
The account is getting maximum value from their current tier
and showing growth signals. They are likely hitting limitations
that the next tier would resolve.
rules.action:
Create Signal with type = "Feature Ceiling Hit"
Set signal.direction = "expansion"
Recommended play: "Tier upgrade conversation — frame
around unlocking value, not increasing spend. Lead with
the 2-3 features they'd gain that map to their usage
patterns."

rules.name: account-health-score
rules.prose:
The health score is a weighted composite of four dimensions:

  - **Product adoption** (30%): Based on usage.adoption_score
    averaged over last 3 months. Higher adoption = getting
    more value = healthier.

  - **Engagement quality** (25%): Based on interaction recency,
    frequency, and sentiment. Weighted toward recent interactions.
    Executive engagement counts 2x.

  - **Support health** (20%): Inverse of open ticket count and
    average resolution time. Accounts with many open negative
    tickets score low.

  - **Commercial signals** (25%): Seat utilization, NRR trend,
    on-time payments. An expanding account scores high; a
    contracting one scores low.

  Scores are recalculated daily. A drop of more than 15 points
  in a single week triggers a CSM alert regardless of absolute score.

rules.name: contact-engagement-score
rules.prose:
Based on the contact's interaction history:
  - Recency: When was their last interaction? (exponential decay)
  - Frequency: How often do they engage per month?
  - Depth: Are they just attending meetings or actively driving
    discussions (organizer vs attendee, sender vs recipient)?
  - Trend: Is engagement increasing or decreasing?

  A contact with high engagement_score who suddenly drops is
  a churn signal. A contact with increasing engagement who
  has seniority >= director is an expansion signal.

rules.name: champion-strength
rules.prose:
Only computed for contacts with role = "champion".
  Based on:
  - Internal advocacy actions (feature requests, case study
    participation, reference calls)
  - Executive access (can they get us meetings with VPs/C-level?)
  - Adoption driving (did usage increase in their department?)
  - Public advocacy (social mentions, conference talks, reviews)

  A champion_strength > 0.8 is a "power champion" — these
  accounts almost never churn. Below 0.3 means the champion
  designation should be re-evaluated.

rules.name: adoption-score
rules.prose:
Calculated as: count(features_used) / count(features_entitled)

  Not all features are weighted equally. "Aha moment" features
  (identified from historical activation analysis) count 3x:
  - Workflow automation
  - Custom dashboards
  - API integration
  - Team collaboration features

rules.name: usage-trend
rules.prose:
Compare current period's active_users against the average
  of the prior 3 periods:
  - Growing: current > avg * 1.1 (>10% increase)
  - Declining: current < avg * 0.8 (<20% decrease)
  - Stable: everything else

rules.name: seat-utilization
rules.prose:
Simple ratio: seats_active / seats_purchased

  Updated weekly. Uses 30-day active definition (at least
  one meaningful session in the last 30 days).

rules.name: opportunity-probability
rules.prose:
Base probability by stage:
  - Discovery: 0.10
  - Qualification: 0.25
  - Evaluation: 0.50
  - Negotiation: 0.75
  - Closed-won: 1.00
  - Closed-lost: 0.00

  Modifiers:
  - Champion identified: +0.15
  - Executive sponsor engaged: +0.10
  - Competitor active in deal: -0.15
  - Previous expansion history: +0.10
  - Budget confirmed: +0.10
  - Blocker identified: -0.10

  Capped at [0.05 .. 0.95] to avoid false certainty.

rules.name: active-seats
rules.prose:
Count of unique users associated with this subscription
  who had at least one meaningful session in the last 30 days.
  A "meaningful session" is defined as: logged in AND performed
  at least one action beyond viewing the dashboard.

taxonomies.name: ProductCatalog
taxonomies.description: Our full product portfolio organized by category and tier. This taxonomy drives whitespace analysis — comparing what an account has versus what's available — and informs the expansion agent's product recommendations.

children.name: Starter
children.description: $29/seat/month. Our entry point for teams under 20. Core dashboard, basic reporting, 5 integrations max. Most common entry: self-serve trial (14-day, no credit card). Conversion path: trial → monthly → annual after 3 months. Key limitation: no workflow automation, no API access. When accounts hit these walls, they're upgrade-ready.

children.name: Professional
children.description: $79/seat/month. Mid-market workhorse. Everything in Starter plus advanced analytics, workflow automation, custom fields, API access (10K calls/day), and 25 integrations. This is where the majority of expansion revenue concentrates. Accounts that adopt workflow automation have 3x retention. Key limitation: no SSO, no audit logs, no SLA. Ceiling signals: > 50 users, security review requests, procurement team involvement.

children.name: Enterprise
children.description: $149/seat/month (annual contracts only, minimum 50 seats). Everything in Professional plus SSO/SAML, audit logging, SLA (99.95% uptime), dedicated CSM, unlimited integrations, API (100K calls/day), custom data retention policies. Average sales cycle: 45 days. Requires champion + exec sponsor. Enterprise accounts rarely churn (< 3% annual rate). Key signal for upgrade: procurement/security team engagement.

children.name: Advanced Analytics
children.description: $20/seat/month. Available on Professional and Enterprise. Predictive dashboards, cohort analysis, funnel visualization, custom SQL queries, scheduled report delivery. Accounts with this add-on have 40% lower churn — it's our single strongest stickiness feature. When you see an account exporting CSVs to do analysis in Sheets, that's the signal to pitch Analytics. Prerequisite behavior: heavy use of basic reporting for 2+ months.

children.name: Data Export
children.description: $500/month flat fee (not per-seat). Available on Professional and Enterprise. Real-time sync to Snowflake, BigQuery, or Redshift. Signals deep platform commitment — once data flows into their warehouse, switching cost is extreme. Accounts with Data Export: 95% retention rate. Prerequisite behavior: API usage > 5K calls/day OR data_volume > 10GB OR mention of "data warehouse" in support.

children.name: Implementation
children.description: $15K-$75K one-time, scoped by complexity. Standard (< 50 users): $15K, 3 weeks. Advanced (50-200 users, integrations): $35K, 6 weeks. Enterprise (200+ users, migration, custom): $75K, 12 weeks. Accounts that buy implementation onboard 3x faster and have 25% higher adoption scores at 90 days.

children.name: Training
children.description: $3K-$10K per session, virtual or on-site. Admin training (2 days): for the team managing the platform. End-user training (1 day): for daily users. Champion enablement (half day): specifically for advocates — teaches them how to drive adoption internally. Recurring quarterly training correlates with 20% higher NRR.

children.name: Migration
children.description: $20K-$100K, scoped by data complexity and source system. Common sources: Salesforce, HubSpot, legacy custom systems. Migration includes data mapping, validation, parallel run, and cutover support. Migration accounts have highest first-year retention (98%) because the switching cost becomes real.

taxonomies.inheritance_rules:
Products tagged @tag: entry-tier should trigger upgrade analysis
when accounts show ceiling-hit signals (seat utilization > 85%,
feature adoption > 80%, or explicit feature requests for
capabilities in the next tier).

Products tagged @tag: services are not recurring revenue but
strongly correlate with retention. The agent should recommend
services when an account shows adoption struggles (adoption_score
< 0.5 at 60 days) or when a new enterprise deal closes.

Products tagged @tag: analytics or @tag: data-platform are
stickiness multipliers. The agent should proactively recommend
these when usage patterns suggest the account is doing manually
what the add-on automates.

taxonomies.name: SignalType
taxonomies.description: Classification of signals that indicate account trajectory changes. Each signal type has a default direction (expansion or contraction) and historical predictive value.

children.name: Usage Spike
children.description: Active users or sessions increased >30% vs prior period.

children.name: New Department Adoption
children.description: Users from a previously inactive department started using the product. Indicates organic spread.

children.name: Feature Ceiling Hit
children.description: Account is at >90% of a plan limit (seats, API calls, storage). Natural upgrade trigger.

children.name: Executive Engagement
children.description: VP+ level contact initiated engagement (meeting, email, event attendance). Signals strategic interest.

children.name: Expansion Request
children.description: Contact explicitly asked about adding seats, products, or upgrading. Highest-intent signal.

children.name: Integration Deepening
children.description: New API integrations deployed or data volume increased significantly. Indicates platform stickiness.

children.name: Positive Case Study
children.description: Account agreed to be a reference or case study. Strong indicator of satisfaction and advocacy.

children.name: Hiring Signal
children.description: Account is hiring roles that would use our product. Sourced from job board monitoring.

children.name: Usage Decline
children.description: Active users or sessions decreased >20% vs prior period.

children.name: Champion Departure
children.description: Contact with role=champion changed jobs or became inactive. Single strongest churn predictor.

children.name: Support Escalation
children.description: Support ticket escalated to management or unresolved for >5 business days with negative sentiment.

children.name: Competitor Mention
children.description: Competitor name mentioned in support ticket, email, or meeting transcript.

children.name: Seat Underutilization
children.description: Seat utilization dropped below 50%. Account is paying for seats nobody uses.

children.name: Billing Issue
children.description: Payment failed, invoice disputed, or billing support ticket opened.

children.name: Executive Disengagement
children.description: No executive-level interaction in 90+ days for an enterprise account.

children.name: Budget/Reorg Signal
children.description: News of layoffs, reorg, or budget cuts at the account. Sourced from news monitoring.

children.name: Renewal Approaching
children.description: Subscription renewal within 90 days. Not inherently positive or negative but requires attention.

children.name: Stakeholder Change
children.description: Key contact changed role or new contact joined. Could go either direction.

children.name: Product Update Relevant
children.description: We shipped a feature the account had requested. Re-engagement opportunity.

taxonomies.inheritance_rules:
Signals tagged @tag: product-led can be detected purely from
telemetry data — no human input required. These should run
on automated daily detection.

Signals tagged @tag: relationship require NLP analysis of
interaction content (emails, transcripts, tickets). These
run on interaction ingestion events.

Signals tagged @tag: external require third-party data
sources (job boards, news feeds). These run weekly.

glossary.definition:
The annualized value of all active subscriptions for an
account. Calculated as monthly recurring revenue × 12.
Excludes one-time fees, professional services, and
overages. ARR is our primary revenue metric.

Do NOT confuse with ACV (Annual Contract Value), which
includes one-time fees and is used for deal sizing.

Trailing 12-month revenue from existing accounts divided
by the revenue those same accounts had 12 months ago.
Includes expansion, contraction, and churn.
NRR > 100% means existing accounts are growing net of churn.

Formula: (Starting ARR + Expansion - Contraction - Churn) / Starting ARR

Do NOT confuse with GRR (Gross Revenue Retention), which
excludes expansion and only measures contraction + churn.

Net new ARR from existing customers. Sources include:
seat expansion, tier upgrades, and add-on product purchases.
Does NOT include price increases at renewal — those are
classified as "pricing uplift" and tracked separately.

Decrease in ARR from an existing account that remains a
customer. Causes include: seat reduction, tier downgrade,
or removal of an add-on product. An account can contract
without churning.

Complete loss of an account's revenue. All subscriptions
cancelled or not renewed. Measured as churned ARR / total
ARR at start of period. Churn is calculated at the account
level, not the subscription level.

Voluntary churn: customer chose to leave.
Involuntary churn: payment failure, account closure.

Products or capabilities in our catalog that a customer
does not currently subscribe to but could benefit from.
Whitespace analysis compares the account's current
subscriptions against the full product catalog, weighted
by the account's usage patterns and industry benchmarks.

A contact at the customer who actively advocates for our
product internally. Champions drive adoption, defend budget
during cuts, and expand usage to new teams. Identified by:
high engagement score, proactive outreach, feature request
frequency, and willingness to do references.

A champion is NOT just a power user. A power user uses the
product a lot; a champion influences others to use it too.

The person who controls the budget and signs contracts.
Often different from the champion. In enterprise accounts,
the economic buyer is typically VP+ level while the champion
may be a director or senior manager.

The firmographic and behavioral profile of accounts most
likely to become and remain successful customers. Our ICP
is defined by: industry, employee count, tech stack,
use case alignment, and growth trajectory. ICP fit score
measures how closely an account matches.

A composite metric (0-100) reflecting the overall health of
the customer relationship. See rules/scoring.lore#account-health-score
for the calculation methodology. Health score is a lagging
indicator — it reflects what has already happened. Signals
are the leading indicators of where health is heading.

A detected event or pattern that predicts a change in
account trajectory. Signals are the primary input to the
expansion and churn detection engines. See
taxonomies/signal-types.lore for the full classification.

A recommended sequence of actions in response to a signal
or account state. Plays are prescriptive — they tell the
CSM or AE what to do, who to contact, and what messaging
to use. Plays are NOT fully automated; they require human
judgment and execution.

The set of accounts owned by a CSM or AE. Measured by
total ARR, account count, and weighted pipeline. Used
for workload balancing and territory planning.

views.name: Account Executive
views.description: Scoped perspective for Account Executives focused on expansion pipeline, deal progression, and revenue growth.
entities: Account (segment, arr, stage, health_score, icp_fit_score, nrr_trailing_12m)
Contact (name, title, role, seniority, engagement_score, champion_strength)
Signal (type, strength, direction, recommended_play WHERE direction = expansion)
key_questions: Which accounts in my book have the highest expansion potential?
What signals have fired for my accounts this week?
Where is there whitespace I haven't pursued?
What's the recommended play for this expansion signal?
How does this account's parent company rollup look?
Which deals need champion identification?
views.not_in_scope:
Support ticket details, detailed usage telemetry, and churn
intervention workflows. AEs see health scores and signals
but detailed churn management is the CSM's domain.

views.name: Customer Success Manager
views.description: Scoped perspective for CSMs focused on account health, churn prevention, adoption, and renewal management.
key_questions: Which accounts need immediate attention?
What's driving this account's health score change?
Who is the champion and how strong is their advocacy?
What does the engagement timeline look like?
Is this account at risk for renewal?
What happened that triggered this churn signal?
Which accounts have declining usage trends?
views.not_in_scope:
Deal negotiation details, discount approvals, and
competitive pricing intelligence. CSMs flag expansion
opportunities but hand off deal execution to AEs.

views.name: RevOps
views.description: Full ontology view for Revenue Operations. RevOps sees everything — this view is the complete domain model used for analytics, forecasting, and system configuration.
key_questions: What's our overall NRR and what's driving it?
How effective is the AI expansion agent? (signal-to-revenue)
Where are the systemic churn risk patterns?
How should we rebalance CSM book assignments?
What's the forecast for expansion pipeline this quarter?
Which signals have the highest conversion rate to closed-won?
Are there segments where our ICP scoring is miscalibrated?

observations.heading: Workflow automation is the Professional stickiness inflection
observations.prose:
Analyzed 500+ Professional accounts over 18 months. Accounts
that adopt workflow-automation within the first 90 days have
a 92% 12-month retention rate vs. 68% for those who don't.

The adoption-prerequisite chain matters: accounts that go
dashboard -> basic-reporting -> custom-fields -> workflow-automation
(in that order) have the highest success. Accounts that jump
straight to workflow-automation without adopting custom-fields
first tend to build brittle workflows and churn at month 6.

Recommendation: the agent should track Feature adoption sequence,
not just adoption count. Skipped prerequisites are a risk signal.

observations.heading: API access adoption predicts Enterprise readiness
observations.prose:
Accounts on Professional that adopt api-access within their
first 6 months upgrade to Enterprise at 3x the rate of
accounts that don't. The mechanism is clear: API adoption
signals a technical team that's integrating deeply, which
creates both stickiness and the need for Enterprise features
(higher rate limits, SSO for their engineering team).

When the agent sees API usage > 5K calls/day on a Professional
account, it should create an Enterprise upgrade signal AND
a Data Export recommendation simultaneously. These almost
always travel together.

observations.heading: Advanced Analytics has an adoption cliff at 2 weeks
observations.prose:
Of accounts that trial the Analytics add-on, 60% convert if
they build a custom dashboard in the first 2 weeks. After
that, conversion drops to 15%. The "aha moment" is seeing
their own data in a custom dashboard — not the pre-built ones.

Recommendation: when an Analytics trial starts, the agent
should prompt the CSM to schedule a dashboard-building session
within the first week. Don't wait for the customer to figure
it out on their own.

observations.heading: SSO requests are the most reliable Enterprise signal
observations.prose:
In the last year, 85% of Professional accounts that requested
SSO/SAML documentation upgraded to Enterprise within 6 months.
This is our single most reliable upgrade signal — more reliable
than seat count, usage growth, or executive engagement.

The reason: SSO requests come from security/IT teams, which
means the account has crossed from "team tool" to "company
tool." Once IT is involved, they require compliance features
that only Enterprise provides.

The agent should treat any mention of "SSO", "SAML", "single
sign-on", or "security review" in support tickets or meetings
as a strong, immediate Enterprise upgrade signal.

observations.heading: Acme Corp shows expansion readiness
observations.prose:
Acme Corp (acme.com) is showing classic multi-signal expansion
patterns over the past 30 days:

- Active users increased 45%, from 120 to 174
- Three new departments started using the product
- VP Engineering Sarah Chen attended the enterprise webinar
- Support tickets shifted from "how do I" to "can it also do"

This pattern has historically preceded expansion in 78% of
similar enterprise accounts. Recommend AE outreach within
2 weeks, leading with the multi-department adoption story.

observations.heading: Beta Corp usage decline looks seasonal
observations.prose:
Beta Corp (beta.io) showed a 30% usage decline in Q4,
but this matches their industry's seasonal pattern. Three
of their four peer accounts showed similar dips.

This is likely NOT a churn signal. The usage-decline-alert
rule fired here but was a false positive. Consider adding
seasonal adjustment to the rule.

observations.heading: Gamma Inc rapid onboarding
observations.prose:
Gamma Inc onboarded 50 users in their first week, well
above the typical 15-20 for their segment. Their champion
(CTO) is highly engaged and has already asked about API
access and enterprise features.

This looks like a fast-track expansion candidate. The
seat-expansion-trigger rule should fire within 2 weeks
at this adoption rate.

observations.heading: Executive engagement signals are strongest predictor
observations.prose:
After analyzing 200+ expansion outcomes over the past year,
executive engagement (webinar attendance, feature requests from
VP+ titles) is the single strongest predictor of expansion,
outperforming usage metrics by a significant margin.

Current signal weighting in the expansion detection rules
underweights executive engagement relative to raw usage growth.
Recommend rebalancing.

observations.heading: Usage decline signals need industry context
observations.prose:
The usage-decline-alert rule fires uniformly across all
account segments, but decline patterns vary significantly
by industry. Retail accounts show predictable seasonal dips
in Q1 and Q4. SaaS accounts show usage decline during
holiday periods but recover quickly.

Without industry context, the false positive rate for
usage decline signals is approximately 35% for enterprise
accounts in cyclical industries.

outcomes.heading: Acme Corp expansion — correct
outcomes.prose:
We predicted Acme Corp was expansion-ready (confidence: 0.82).
They expanded. Upsell closed 2025-06-28 for +$45K ARR,
upgrading from Growth to Enterprise tier.

The VP Engineering engagement was the strongest signal —
she championed the enterprise tier internally after the
webinar. The multi-department adoption pattern confirmed
product-market fit at the organization level.
takeaways: increase weight of executive engagement signals in the expansion detection rules
multi-department adoption should be a first-class signal type in the Signal entity

outcomes.heading: Beta Corp churn risk — false positive
outcomes.prose:
We flagged Beta Corp as churn risk (confidence: 0.68).
They renewed at the same tier. The usage decline was
seasonal, as our observation suspected.

This is the third Q4 false positive from the
usage-decline-alert rule in enterprise accounts in
cyclical industries.
takeaways: the usage-decline-alert rule needs seasonal adjustment for cyclical industries
Account entity should track industry vertical to enable seasonal pattern detection

outcomes.heading: Gamma Inc fast expansion — correct
outcomes.prose:
We identified Gamma Inc as a fast-track expansion candidate.
They upgraded to the next tier within 3 weeks of onboarding,
adding 30 more seats.

The rapid onboarding velocity was the key signal. CTO
engagement and API access requests confirmed enterprise intent.
takeaways: rapid onboarding velocity (3x segment average) should trigger immediate AE notification
API access requests correlate strongly with enterprise expansion intent
